stem:
A water tank is draining at a constant rate. The table below shows the volume of water $V$, in gallons, remaining in the tank at time $t$, in hours.

| $t$ (hours) | $V$ (gallons) |
|---|---|
| 2 | 620 |
| 6 | 380 |

Assuming $V$ is a linear function of $t$, at what time $t$ will the tank contain exactly 140 gallons?
explanation: Step 1: Find the slope (rate of change). Using the two table values: $$m = \frac{380 - 620}{6 - 2} = \frac{-240}{4} = -60 \text{ gallons per hour}$$ Step 2: Find the $V$-intercept $b$ using the point $(2, 620)$: $$620 = -60(2) + b \implies 620 = -120 + b \implies b = 740$$ So $V(t) = -60t + 740$. Step 3: Solve for $t$ when $V = 140$: $$-60t + 740 = 140 \implies -60t = -600 \implies t = 10$$ The tank contains 140 gallons at $t = 10$ hours.

distractorRationale.A: Uses the total change in volume (240) as the rate without dividing by the time interval, giving slope $-240$; sets $-240t + 1100 = 140$, yielding $-240t = -960$, so $t = 4$.
distractorRationale.B: Uses the $t = 2$ table reading as the $V$-intercept directly ($b = 620$ instead of computing the true intercept of 740): solves $-60t + 620 = 140$, giving $-60t = -480$, so $t = 8$.
distractorRationale.D: Computes the slope as $-240 \div 6 = -40$ (divides by $t = 6$ rather than the time difference $6 - 2 = 4$); finds $b = 620 - (-40)(2) = 700$; solves $-40t + 700 = 140$, giving $-40t = -560$, so $t = 14$.
